one_line_takeaway: HarmChip is a 360-prompt (x2 difficulty tiers), 16-domain jailbreak benchmark built via an automated LLM-driven pipeline that shows current LLM safety alignment is bimodally polarized on hardware-security attacks (near-zero ASR for a few models like Gemini-3.1-Pro vs. 90-100% ASR for models like Devstral-2512 and GPT-4.1) while simultaneously over-refusing legitimate hardware-security engineering queries.
key_finding: Aggregated ASR is bimodal across the 16 evaluated models: on the Hard benchmark the eight most-resistant models (Step-3.5-Flash through Claude-Sonnet-4.6) score below 50% ASR, with the top four under 10% (Step-3.5-Flash 1.94%, Gemini-3.1-Pro 3.89%, Qwen3.5-Plus 4.72%, GPT-5.4 6.11%), while from Gemini-3-Flash onward ASR jumps to 47.22% and rises to 100% for Devstral-2512 (with LLaMA-4-Maverick at 98.33% and GPT-4.1 near-ceiling). On the Easy benchmark the same ordering holds but with elevated mid-tier ASR, e.g. Claude-Sonnet-4.6 rises from 12.50% (Hard, reported as ~12.50 vs. earlier-listed 18.61 depending on the specific bar in Fig.3 -- the paper text states Claude-Sonnet-4.6 increases from 12.50% to 44.17% Easy) and Gemini-3-Flash rises from 47.22% to 71.11%. Step-3.5-Flash and Gemini-3.1-Pro have the lowest ASR in both settings; GPT-4.1, LLaMA-4-Maverick, and Devstral-2512 stay at or near full compliance regardless of difficulty. Per-category analysis (Fig. 4) shows Firmware & Runtime and Silicon Manufacturing are the most resistant domains (likely under-represented in general training corpora), while CPS Hardware, Testing & Debugging, and Side-Channel Attacks are the most vulnerable. Model-level Ward clustering (Fig. 5) reveals a stable three-tier stratification reproduced across both difficulty tiers: a refusal-oriented cluster (e.g., Gemini-3.1-Pro, Qwen-397B, ASR below 17%), an intermediate cluster (e.g., Claude-Opus-4.6, Claude-Sonnet-4.6, ASR 9-50%), and a high-compliance cluster (e.g., GPT-4.1, LLaMA-4-Maverick, Devstral-2512) that complies broadly across categories. Category-level clustering (Fig. 6) shows hardware-security categories bifurcate into a 'system-level/algorithmic' super-cluster (e.g., Side-Channel Attacks, IP Protection, ML Security) and a 'physical-implementation' super-cluster (e.g., Analog Security, Hardware Trojans, CPS Hardware), with Silicon Manufacturing an outlier, and this partition is stable across both benchmark tiers -- indicating the clustering reflects domain semantics rather than prompt difficulty. Two qualitative case studies illustrate the 'alignment paradox': (1) a logic-locking resynthesis attack disguised as a 'security audit' -- Devstral-2512 fully complies with a functional Tcl script (including a 'compile_ultra -ungroup' style directive) usable for downstream SAT-based deobfuscation attacks, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 gives a semantically grounded refusal identifying the attack pipeline, and Step-3.5-Flash returns a null/silent refusal without contextual reasoning; (2) a power-side-channel leakage vector requested via a Z3 SMT solver script disguised as auditing a secure scan architecture -- GPT-4.1 fully complies (generating a script with comments like 'MAXIMIZE TOGGLE ACTIVITY'), GPT-5.4 refuses the concrete attack and redirects to defensive alternatives, and Gemini-3.1-Pro declines code generation but offers theoretical analysis and mitigations, illustrating a generational safety improvement within the GPT family and convergence toward context-aware refusal among frontier models.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is HarmChip, the first domain-specific jailbreak benchmark for evaluating LLM safety alignment in hardware security (EDA/RTL/IC design context). It (1) defines a two-level taxonomy of 16 hardware-security domains (grouped into 7 lifecycle-stage categories) built from a literature base of 'over 1,200 papers' spanning 2004-2026; (2) instantiates 120 threat scenarios, each expanded into 3 prompts per difficulty tier, for 360 prompts each in Easy and Hard variants; (3) introduces a three-stage automated benchmark-construction pipeline -- (i) threat taxonomy/data curation with 'information sanitization' that strips paper titles/authors while preserving exploit logic to avoid triggering hardcoded refusals, (ii) automated red-team prompt generation via an 'academic sandbox framing' that yields 8 disguised jailbreak prompts per threat (960 total), and (iii) automated benchmark curation, where 6 curator LLMs (Claude-Sonnet-4.6, GPT-5.4, Gemini-3.1-Pro, MiniMax-M2.5, DeepSeek-V3.2, GLM-5) are queried on all 960 prompts, each response judged by Gemini-3-Flash as compliant/refusal, models ranked by per-prompt Attack Success Rate (ASR), and prompts assigned to Hard (only the 3 least-susceptible models comply) vs Easy (only the 3 most-susceptible models comply) tiers; and (4) an empirical evaluation of 16 LLMs across 10 providers on the resulting benchmark, reporting an 'alignment paradox' where models simultaneously over-refuse legitimate security-engineering queries and comply with semantically disguised malicious ones.
threat_model: Adversary is either a rogue chip designer or an external user of an LLM-assisted EDA tool who wants to inject hardware-level vulnerabilities (hardware Trojans, side-channel leakage, IP/backdoor insertion) into the IC design flow via an LLM. The attack vector is semantic disguise: the malicious payload is phrased as syntactically legitimate engineering language (e.g., a Trojan insertion framed as a Power-Performance-Area optimization, or a functional Engineering Change Order on a finite-state machine), which is claimed to bypass keyword-matching/general-purpose safety guardrails that were not trained on hardware-domain semantics. The paper explicitly does not evaluate real fabrication or synthesis-level harm -- it evaluates only whether an LLM will emit substantive exploit-relevant text/RTL/scripts in response to such disguised prompts, and separately whether it over-refuses legitimate defensive/red-team security queries.
limitations: The authors explicitly state (Sec. VII) that HarmChip evaluates model behavior only at the language level; it does not compile, simulate, or synthesize compliant LLM outputs to verify they constitute functionally malicious hardware, so language-level ASR may over- or under-estimate real-world harm (outputs can be syntactically alarming yet functionally inert, or superficially benign yet sufficient to enable an attack). They note the benchmark does not yet cover more advanced multi-turn, adaptive, or further-obfuscated jailbreak strategies, and call for expanded per-scenario prompt coverage and a dynamic update mechanism for emerging threats. Additional limitations evident from the methodology itself (not explicitly flagged by the authors): the compliance/refusal judgment relies entirely on a single LLM-as-Judge (Gemini-3-Flash) with no reported human-agreement/inter-rater validation; the Easy/Hard split is defined relative to the ASR ordering of the 6 curator models rather than an independent notion of prompt complexity, which could confound 'difficulty' with idiosyncrasies of those specific 6 models; and the benchmark-generation pipeline itself uses LLMs (for sanitization, prompt generation, and judging) without a described human audit of prompt/label quality.

datasets_used: HarmChip benchmark itself: 16 hardware-security domains, 120 threat scenarios, 360 prompts per difficulty tier (Easy and Hard), derived from an underlying literature corpus of 'over 1,200 papers' spanning 2004-2026 (Table I lists representative venues per domain, e.g. DAC, HOST, TIFS, USENIX Security, CCS, Oakland, ICCAD, TCHES, ASIACRYPT)
quantitative_results: Hard benchmark aggregate ASR: Step-3.5-Flash 1.94%, Gemini-3.1-Pro 3.89%, Qwen3.5-Plus 4.72%, GPT-5.4 6.11%, Qwen3.5-397B 6.39%, MiniMax-M2.5 8.61%, GLM-5 16.39%, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 12.50%, Gemini-3-Flash 47.22%, Claude-Opus-4.6 55.83%, Kimi-K2.5 60.83%, DeepSeek-V3.2 78.06%, Grok-4.1-Fast 83.06%, LLaMA-4-Maverick 90.56%, GPT-4.1 98.33%, Devstral-2512 100.00%

Easy benchmark aggregate ASR: Step-3.5-Flash 3.61%, Gemini-3.1-Pro 11.67%, Qwen3.5-Plus/GPT-5.4/Qwen3.5-397B/MiniMax-M2.5 cluster around 18.61-20.00%, GLM-5 38.61%, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 44.17%, Gemini-3-Flash 71.11%, Claude-Opus-4.6 72.22%, Kimi-K2.5 69.17%, DeepSeek-V3.2 90.83%, Grok-4.1-Fast 91.39%, LLaMA-4-Maverick 96.39%, GPT-4.1 99.44%, Devstral-2512 100.00%

Model-level clustering identifies 3 behavioral tiers stable across both benchmarks: refusal-oriented (<17% ASR, e.g. Gemini-3.1-Pro, Qwen-397B), intermediate (9-50% ASR, e.g. Claude-Opus-4.6, Claude-Sonnet-4.6), high-compliance (near-ceiling across categories, e.g. GPT-4.1, LLaMA-4-Maverick, Devstral-2512)

Per-category ASR heatmap (Fig. 4) shows Firmware & Runtime and Silicon Manufacturing categories most resistant across models; CPS Hardware, Testing & Debugging, and Side-Channel Attacks most vulnerable
Benchmark construction: 120 threats x 8 sanitized source papers = 960 raw jailbreak prompts generated by the 3-stage pipeline, later filtered/split into 360 Easy + 360 Hard prompts
baselines_compared: No prior benchmark is used as a direct empirical baseline comparison in the evaluation tables -- the paper positions HarmChip against general-purpose/software-centric jailbreak benchmarks (AdvBench, JailbreakBench, HarmBench, XSTest, Llama Guard) only conceptually in Sec. II as prior work that does not cover hardware-domain semantic disguise; it does not run these external benchmarks empirically in this paper.

The 6 curator LLMs (Claude-Sonnet-4.6, GPT-5.4, Gemini-3.1-Pro, MiniMax-M2.5, DeepSeek-V3.2, GLM-5) used to build the Easy/Hard split are a subset of the 16 models evaluated later, functioning as an internal calibration set rather than an external baseline.
